title: Ensure end-to-end TLS encryption is enabled

input_row.title: Ensure end-to-end TLS encryption is enabled
input_row.description: End-to-end (E2E) TLS encryption ensures that front-end to worker communication within App Service deployment slots is encrypted using TLS. Without this feature, while incoming HTTPS requests are encrypted to the front ends, the traffic from front ends to workers running the application workloads would travel unencrypted inside Azure’s infrastructure.
input_row.rationale: E2E TLS helps ensure full encryption of traffic between: • Clients and front ends • Front ends and worker processes hosting the application
input_row.audit: Audit from Azure Portal 1. Go to App Services. 2. Click the name of an app. 3. Under Deployment, click Deployment slots. 4. Click the name of a deployment slot. 5. Under Settings, click Configuration. 6. In the General settings pane, under Platform settings, ensure that Enable end-to-end TLS encryption is set to On. 7. Repeat steps 1-6 for each app and deployment slot. Audit from Azure CLI Run the following command to list apps: az webapp list For each app, run the following command to list deployment slots: az webapp deployment slot list --resource-group --name For each deployment slot, ensure that endToEndEncryptionEnabled is set to true. Audit from Azure Policy If referencing a digital copy of this Benchmark, clicking a Policy ID will open a link to the associated Policy definition in Azure. If referencing a printed copy, you can search Policy IDs from this URL: https://portal.azure.com/#view/Microsoft_Azure_Policy/PolicyMenuBlade/~/Definitions • Policy ID: 123aed70-491a-4f07-a569-e1f3a8dd651e - Name: 'App Service app slots should enable end to end encryption'
input_row.remediation: Remediate from Azure Portal 1. Go to App Services. 2. Click the name of an app. 3. Under Deployment, click Deployment slots. 4. Click the name of a deployment slot. 5. Under Settings, click Configuration. 6. In the General settings pane, under Platform settings, next to Enable end-to-end TLS encryption, click the radio button next to On. 7. Click Save. 8. Click Continue. 9. Repeat steps 1-8 for each app and deployment slot requiring remediation. Remediate from Azure CLI For each deployment slot requiring remediation, run the following command to enable end-to-end TLS encryption: az resource update --resource-group --name /slots/ --resource-type "Microsoft.Web/sites" -- set properties.endToEndEncryptionEnabled=true
input_row.impact: Enabling end-to-end TLS encryption may introduce minimal latency and require additional configuration of certificates and HTTPS settings to ensure compatibility.
input_row.default_value: By default, end-to-end TLS encryption is disabled.
input_row.justification: The control requires verification of end-to-end TLS encryption within an application environment, which is specific to Azure's infrastructure and does not have a direct equivalent in AWS Elastic Beanstalk or API Gateway. AWS does not provide a straightforward API or CLI command to verify end-to-end TLS encryption across all components of an application environment as described. Instead, this requires manual inspection of the configuration of associated resources like load balancers and application settings.
input_row.manual_summary: 1. Log in to the AWS Management Console. 2. Navigate to the Elastic Beanstalk service. 3. Select the application environment you wish to inspect. 4. Check the configuration of the associated load balancer: - Go to the EC2 service. - Select "Load Balancers" from the left-hand menu. - Find the load balancer associated with your Elastic Beanstalk environment. - Verify that HTTPS listeners are configured and that the SSL certificate is correctly applied. 5. Ensure that all traffic between the load balancer and the application instances is encrypted: - Check the security group settings to ensure only HTTPS traffic is allowed. - Verify the application settings to ensure that it only accepts HTTPS connections. 6. Repeat these steps for each application environment and associated resources.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Manual

**MANUAL STEPS:**
1. **Log in to the Azure Portal.**
   - Navigate to the App Services: Search for “App Services” in the search bar and select it.

2. **Select an Application.**
   - Click on the name of the application you wish to inspect from the list of available apps.

3. **Access Deployment Slots.**
   - Under the "Deployment" section in the menu, click on “Deployment slots”.

4. **Select a Deployment Slot.**
   - Click the name of a specific deployment slot you want to verify.

5. **Navigate to Configuration Settings.**
   - Under the “Settings” section, click on “Configuration”.

6. **Verify End-to-End TLS Encryption Setting.**
   - In the General settings pane, locate the setting for “Enable end-to-end TLS encryption”. Ensure that it is set to "On".

7. **Repeat Steps for Each App and Deployment Slot.**
   - Proceed to repeat these steps for each application and deployment slot within your Azure environment that requires inspection.

**Expected Secure Configuration:**
- The setting for “Enable end-to-end TLS encryption” should be enabled (set to "On").

**How to Confirm Compliance:**
- Verify that the configuration matches the expected secure setting (enabled) across all apps and deployment slots. If any of the applications or deployment slots do not have this setting enabled, it indicates non-compliance with the control.
step2_review:
**APPROACH VALIDATION:** PARTIALLY_AGREE

- The classification as Manual is partially correct. While the manual steps are valid, this check can be automated using Azure CLI or Azure SDK for Python. Azure CLI can be used to query the configuration settings of App Services and their deployment slots to verify if end-to-end TLS encryption is enabled. This would be more efficient and scalable, especially in environments with numerous applications and deployment slots.

- Azure CLI Command Example:
  ```bash
  az webapp config show --resource-group <resource-group-name> --name <app-service-name> --query "properties.ftpsState"
  ```

- Azure SDK for Python Example:
  ```python
  from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
  from azure.mgmt.web import WebSiteManagementClient

  credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
  client = WebSiteManagementClient(credential, "<subscription-id>")

  app_service = client.web_apps.get_configuration("<resource-group-name>", "<app-service-name>")
  print(app_service.ftps_state)
  ```

**TECHNICAL ACCURACY:** 7

- The manual steps are accurate and complete for verifying the TLS setting through the Azure Portal.
- However, the assessment lacks automation details, which could be implemented using Azure CLI or Azure SDK for Python.
- The program name is not provided in the original assessment. A descriptive name could be `azure_app_service_tls_in_transit_check`.

**IMPROVEMENTS:**

- **Automation:** Incorporate Azure CLI or Azure SDK for Python to automate the verification process. This will save time and reduce the potential for human error.
  
- **Program Name:** Suggest using a more descriptive name for the automation script, such as `azure_app_service_tls_in_transit_check`, to clearly indicate the security check being performed.

- **Azure Policy:** Consider using Azure Policy to enforce this configuration across all App Services, ensuring compliance is maintained automatically.

**CONFIDENCE:** MEDIUM

- The manual steps are clear, but the lack of automation reduces confidence in the scalability and efficiency of the current approach.

**FINAL RECOMMENDATION:** Modify

- Implement automation using Azure CLI or Azure SDK for Python to check the TLS setting across all App Services and deployment slots.
- Consider re-classifying the approach to Automated, as the capability exists to perform these checks programmatically.
- Provide a clear and descriptive program name for any scripts or automation tools used.

step3_final_decision.decision_text:
**FINAL APPROACH:** Automated

**JUSTIFICATION:**
The task of verifying and ensuring end-to-end TLS encryption for Azure App Services can be automated using Azure CLI or Azure SDK for Python. Both tools provide the capability to query the configuration settings of App Services and their deployment slots, making the process more efficient and scalable, especially in environments with numerous applications and deployment slots. The second AI opinion correctly identifies the potential for automation, which aligns with Azure's capabilities.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

---

**PROGRAM NAME:** 
azure_app_service_deployment_slot_tls_encryption_check

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- Azure Resource/Service: App Services
- API/CLI Method: Azure CLI (`az webapp config show`) or Azure SDK for Python (`WebSiteManagementClient.get_configuration`)
- Properties to Check: `properties.ftpsState` or equivalent property indicating TLS encryption status
- Pass Criteria: The property should indicate that end-to-end TLS encryption is enabled (e.g., `ftpsState` set to "Enabled").
- Fail Criteria: The property indicates that end-to-end TLS encryption is not enabled (e.g., `ftpsState` not set to "Enabled").

**DISAGREEMENTS RESOLVED:**
The first AI opinion suggested a manual approach, while the second AI opinion highlighted the potential for automation. Given the capabilities of Azure CLI and Azure SDK for Python to access and verify configuration settings programmatically, automation is feasible and preferable for efficiency and scalability.

**SOURCE:** CIS_Microsoft_Azure_Compute_Services_Benchmark_v2_0_0
